sentence: Oral lichen planus is a common chronic condition, usually caused by fungal infections in the mouth. It presents as white or gray-white reticular patches on the oral mucosa with clear boundaries, thin and soft in texture, and may be accompanied by mild pain or burning sensation. Treatment for oral lichen planus typically includes the use of antifungal medications, improvement of oral hygiene, and changing some unhealthy habits such as quitting smoking and reducing alcohol intake.

In this case, there are several irregular yellow-brown patches visible on the skin, with significant hyperkeratosis. Combining the clinical presentation and histopathological characteristics, the diagnosis of seborrheic keratosis can be confirmed. Seborrheic keratosis is a common chronic skin condition characterized by abnormal skin keratinization, typically appearing on the face, chest, and back. The etiology may be related to excessive sebum production, excessive keratinization, etc. It generally does not require specific treatment and symptoms can be relieved by maintaining skin hygiene and avoiding irritation.

Based on the photo showing multiple annular erythema on bilateral upper limbs upon extension, along with the patient's medical history and clinical presentation, the diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is confirmed. SLE is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by symptoms such as skin rash, arthritis, and renal involvement. Treatment typically involves the use of corticosteroids and immunosuppressants for control.

The red patches with central atrophy and raised edges on the lower limbs are consistent with the clinical presentation of panniculitis. Panniculitis is a skin condition characterized by local redness, swelling, heat, pain, and sometimes exudate. It commonly occurs on the lower limbs and can be misdiagnosed as other skin conditions. A definitive diagnosis requires a comprehensive evaluation of clinical symptoms, physical signs, and skin biopsy results.

The back photo shows linear brownish patches with mild local atrophy, consistent with the clinical presentation of scleroderma. Scleroderma is a connective tissue disease that can manifest as skin hardening, erythema, edema, pigmentation, and in severe cases, it can involve internal organs.

On this photo, there are diffuse red patches with ulceration, exudation, and crusting, which are consistent with eczema. Eczema is a common skin inflammation usually caused by skin allergies or irritation, commonly found on the elbows, knees, and neck. Treatment for eczema typically involves topical corticosteroid creams, moisturizers, and avoiding triggers. Timely identification and management of eczema can effectively alleviate symptoms and reduce the recurrence rate.

On this photo, there are streak-like distributed brownish papules on the trunk area, forming patches with clear borders. This is a common skin lesion known as epidermal nevus. Epidermal nevus is a benign skin condition that usually does not require treatment, but regular monitoring for changes is recommended.

This is a picture of skin lesions on the feet, showing symptoms such as concave nails, nail detachment, thickening under the nails, loss of gloss on the nail plate, and whitening in all ten toes. These manifestations are consistent with the characteristics of psoriasis. Psoriasis is a chronic, non-communicable skin disease characterized by excessive growth of skin cells, leading to local skin thickening and scaling. This condition may affect the nails, causing nail abnormalities such as nail pitting and nail detachment. It is recommended to seek medical attention for further diagnosis and treatment.

Becker's nevus, also known as pigmented hairy epidermal nevus, is a common skin condition characterized by the presence of hyperpigmented patches with coarse hair growth. It usually appears during adolescence and is more common in males. Becker's nevus is typically benign and does not require treatment unless for cosmetic reasons. However, in some cases, individuals may seek treatment for excessive hair growth or darkening of the pigmented area. Treatment options include laser therapy, topical medications, and hair removal techniques. It is important to protect the skin from sun exposure to prevent exacerbation of the condition.

The patient presents with similar colored papules within the hairline, further assessment involving medical history and auxiliary examinations is necessary to establish a definitive diagnosis. This may include details on the location, morphology, accompanying symptoms of the skin lesions. Common conditions that can cause such papules within the hairline include folliculitis, eczema, dermatitis, among others. Once a diagnosis is confirmed, appropriate treatment plans can be implemented, such as topical or oral medications.

The patient presents with round erythematous patches with central vesicles, erosions, and crusts, suggesting a diagnosis of erythema multiforme. Erythema multiforme is a common skin condition characterized by irregularly shaped red patches that may have central vesicles, erosions, or crusts. This condition is often triggered by allergic reactions, infections, or medications. Treatment options include observation, topical medications, and oral antihistamines if necessary.

Based on the description of the medical history, with red papules on the lateral side of the toes and purple ecchymosis at the edges, the possible diagnosis to consider is granuloma. Granuloma is an inflammatory condition usually caused by abnormal tissue reaction to certain stimuli or infections, presenting with local redness, swelling, pain, and possibly purple ecchymosis. Further clinical and laboratory examinations are needed for a definitive diagnosis.

The characteristics of yellow-brown oval papules, elevated, firm, and movable at the base are consistent with the clinical presentation of neurofibroma. Neurofibroma is a common type of nerve sheath tumor that is usually benign and originates from nerve tissue, often occurring in the nerve sheath of the skin and other tissues. After diagnosing neurofibroma, further evaluation of the size, location, and potential impact of the tumor is needed to develop an appropriate treatment plan.
Patients present with red papules on the trunk with black spots on the edges. Skin tumor is suspected, and further histopathological examination is needed for a definite diagnosis.

Based on the multiple isolated pustules with surrounding erythema seen in the photo of the head and face, the diagnosis of pustular psoriasis is made. Psoriasis is a chronic and recurrent skin disease that belongs to autoimmune diseases. Pustular psoriasis is one of the common clinical variants, characterized by pustular eruptions with surrounding erythema and often accompanied by itching.

On this photo, we can see scattered pale red papules and vesicles on the trunk of the patient, some of which merge into patches and appear shiny on the surface. Based on these features, the patient is diagnosed with eosinophilic dermatosis, a chronic and recurrent skin condition that typically presents with symptoms like erythematous patches, papules, vesicles on the skin, often accompanied by itching. Treatment usually involves topical corticosteroids, antihistamines to control symptoms, while severe cases may require oral medications or phototherapy for further management.

The photo shows skin lesions on the right side of the face, characterized by erythema, erosion, crusting, scratch marks, and brownish pigment deposition. Based on the symptoms and signs, a preliminary diagnosis of neurodermatitis is made.

This is a typical presentation of pyogenic granuloma, characterized by an oval-shaped deep ulcer with granulation tissue at the base and red swollen edges. Pyogenic granuloma is a benign soft tissue tumor that commonly occurs on the skin and mucous membranes, often seen in the oral cavity, nasal cavity, and genital areas. The etiology is usually related to chronic irritation, infection, or immune abnormalities. Treatment options include surgical excision, medication, and radiation therapy.

Based on the features observed in the facial skin lesion photo, showing numerous skin-colored to pale red papules and nodules with clear borders that do not merge, the preliminary diagnosis is consistent with sarcoidosis. Sarcoidosis is a chronic non-caseating inflammatory disease that can affect multiple organs, particularly the lungs, lymph nodes, and eyes. Common cutaneous manifestations include papules, nodules, and hyperpigmentation, and a comprehensive evaluation involving clinical presentation and laboratory tests is essential for confirmation.

The photo of the foot shows multiple keratotic pits on the sole, consistent with the clinical presentation of plantar warts. Plantar warts are a common skin condition caused by infection with human papillomavirus (HPV), typically presenting as rough wart-like lesions on the sole or in between the toes. Local treatment is recommended to remove the affected tissue.

The photo shows features of erosions and ulcers on the buccal mucosa. Combined with clinical symptoms and medical history, a preliminary diagnosis of oral squamous cell carcinoma can be made. Oral squamous cell carcinoma is one of the most common malignant tumors in the oral cavity, often found on the surface of the oral mucosa and typically presenting as ulcerative growths. Further confirmation would require additional tissue pathology examination.

Central pore in the skin lesion suggests a possible sebaceous cyst, while the surrounding area of depressed skin with a pale purple color on the surface may be due to vascular damage caused by pressure on the skin. Further evaluation is recommended to confirm the diagnosis and consider the need for surgical treatment.

This is a photo showing symmetrical butterfly-shaped rash on the face, which is a typical presentation of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). SLE is a chronic autoimmune disease that primarily affects the skin, joints, kidneys, and other organs. Common symptoms include facial rash, joint pain, fatigue, and fever. Diagnosis requires a comprehensive evaluation of clinical symptoms, laboratory tests, and skin biopsy.

The patient presents with linear dark red patches on the lower limbs with central white atrophy, possibly indicative of panniculitis. Panniculitis is a skin condition characterized by localized redness, swelling, and pain, often accompanied by white vesicles or ulcers. Further examination and observation are needed for a definitive diagnosis.

The patient presents with orange-yellow papules with surface folds on the upper limbs. Based on the symptoms described, elastic fiber pseudoxanthoma is considered, but further examinations, including histopathological examination, are needed to confirm the diagnosis. Elastic fiber pseudoxanthoma is a rare skin condition characterized by yellowish papules on the skin, requiring histopathological examination for definitive diagnosis.

The scattered red patches of varying sizes on the upper limbs may be due to inflammatory skin changes, and a pathological examination is recommended to confirm the diagnosis, further determine the cause, and formulate a treatment plan. This condition may involve an inflammatory response in the skin, and a tissue biopsy may be needed to observe histopathological changes and confirm the diagnosis.

From the lateral view of the face, red patches can be seen on the auricle and cheek, combined with the patient's symptoms and medical history, highly suspicious of actinic prurigo. Actinic prurigo is an inflammatory skin condition caused by prolonged exposure to ultraviolet radiation, commonly seen on sun-exposed areas such as the face, neck, and hands.

The photo of the shoulder shows diffuse light brown patches with increased hair growth. Based on the characteristics of the lesion and clinical presentation, the preliminary diagnosis is seborrheic keratosis. Seborrheic keratosis is a common benign skin condition that usually does not require treatment, but regular monitoring is necessary.

Erythema multiforme is a common skin condition characterized by scattered red patches on the skin, sometimes with central erosion and crusting. The exact cause of this disease is not clear, but it is believed to be related to factors such as allergic reactions, infections, or autoimmunity. When diagnosing erythema multiforme, doctors consider the patient's clinical symptoms, skin manifestations, and medical history. Treatment for erythema multiforme typically involves topical medications and oral antihistamines.

Linear or patchy skin lesions with itching are observed on the patient's skin, suggesting a possible skin condition caused by nematode infection. Nematode infection may occur due to contact with infectious sources such as parasitic worms in the soil. Further clinical and laboratory investigations are needed for a definite diagnosis to determine the underlying cause and to establish an appropriate treatment plan.

This photograph shows a light brown patch with a superficial ulcer in the center and slightly raised edges, which is a typical presentation of in situ squamous cell carcinoma. In situ squamous cell carcinoma is a localized early-stage squamous cell carcinoma that typically forms on the surface of the skin without invading deeper tissues. This condition can usually be treated with surgical excision and has a good prognosis.

This is a photo of a skin lesion on the anterior chest. It shows patchy erythema with indistinct borders and greasy scales, consistent with the clinical features of seborrheic dermatitis. Seborrheic dermatitis is a common inflammatory skin condition typically caused by an overproduction of sebum from the sebaceous glands. Symptoms may include redness, scaling, and itching at the affected area, and prompt medical treatment and management are recommended.

This chest photo shows linear elevations with a light yellow color, consistent with the features of a lymphatic malformation. Lymphatic malformation is a benign tumor formed by abnormal development of lymphatic vessels, typically presenting as an expansive mass in the skin or soft tissues, commonly found in areas such as the neck and armpits. Lymphatic malformations are usually congenital, mostly solitary, and often asymptomatic, but may enlarge and compress surrounding tissues causing symptoms such as pain or functional impairment. Diagnosis of lymphatic malformation typically requires a combination of clinical presentation, imaging studies, and histopathological examination. Treatment options include surgical excision, sclerosing agent injection, and laser therapy.

Langerhans cell histiocytosis is a rare disease characterized by abnormal proliferation of Langerhans cells in the lymphoid tissues. This condition can present with symptoms such as lymphadenopathy, fever, fatigue, and weight loss. Diagnosis typically involves lymph node biopsy and histopathological examination. Treatment may involve observation, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, or surgery, depending on the severity of the condition and individual factors.

The diagnosis of black keratosis is characterized by chronic non-infectious skin disease, presenting with brown or black papules on the skin with rough and keratotic surface, commonly found on sun-exposed areas. The etiology of black keratosis is not clear, possibly related to genetic factors, sunlight exposure, age, etc. Treatment mainly includes topical medication and laser therapy.

This image shows skin lesions around the eyelid, with mild erosion at the base of the eyelashes, accompanied by exudate and lice bodies, consistent with the clinical presentation of phthiriasis. Phthiriasis is caused by lice parasites infesting the eyelash follicles and is common in environments with poor hygiene conditions. Treatment includes local medication and hygiene education, and surgical intervention may be considered to remove the lice if necessary. Timely diagnosis and treatment are crucial for preventing complications and transmission to others.

The photo shows alternating red and pale white streaks on the back, which are typical features of stretch marks. Stretch marks are a common skin condition that occurs when the skin is stretched and pulled, commonly seen in pregnant women on the abdomen, buttocks, thighs, and breasts. These marks often appear gradually in late pregnancy or postpartum, usually due to inadequate skin elasticity. Prevention methods include maintaining adequate skin hydration and avoiding rapid weight gain.
The patchy wine-red lesion with irregular borders and asymmetric shape may be a skin vascular tumor. It is recommended to undergo a skin dermoscopy examination for further clarification.

The diagnosis is follicular eczema, characterized by brownish follicular keratotic papules that may coalesce into plaques. It is a chronic inflammatory skin condition commonly found on the scalp, face, neck, and chest. The etiology may involve follicular keratosis, sebum overproduction, fungal infections, among other factors. Treatment options include topical medications such as keratolytics, corticosteroid creams, and oral medications for severe cases.

In this photo, there is a pale red round nodule on the lower limb, protruding from the skin surface, preliminary diagnosed as dermatofibroma based on the appearance and characteristics of the lesion. Dermatofibroma is a common benign skin tumor, typically composed of connective tissue and fibrous tissue. This tumor is usually painless, non-metastasizing, but sometimes it can grow and cause discomfort. Treatment typically involves surgical excision with a good prognosis.

This photo shows scattered erythematous papules on the face of a female. Some of the papules are red. Based on the symptoms and appearance, the preliminary diagnosis is acne vulgaris. Acne vulgaris is a common skin condition that typically occurs during adolescence, characterized by comedones, papules, and nodules on the face, chest, and back. Treatment options include topical medications, oral medications, and surgical therapy. Timely treatment can effectively control symptoms and prevent disease progression.

This photo shows a patient with a circular arc-shaped red rash on the leg, with the center appearing dark red, which is characteristic of erythema annulare centrifugum. Erythema annulare centrifugum is a localized skin condition that typically presents as red or purplish-red, with well-defined borders, in a ring-shaped or oval shape, often accompanied by itching or burning sensation. This skin condition may be associated with various factors such as allergic reactions, fungal infections, or autoimmune diseases. It is recommended for the patient to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and treatment.

This is a photo of the forearm showing multiple red flat papules with a target-like lesion in the center, which is typical of erythema multiforme. Erythema multiforme is a self-limiting skin disease characterized by irregular red patches, often accompanied by itching or burning sensation, and sometimes central erosion. Treatment options include topical medications, oral antihistamines, or corticosteroids. Timely diagnosis and treatment are crucial.

Based on the description of the photo showing shiny yellow-brown patches and two ulcerated scabs on the anterior shin, the diagnosis of lipoid progressive necrosis is made. This condition is characterized by skin ischemia and necrosis due to vascular changes. Further investigations are recommended for confirmation of the diagnosis, followed by appropriate treatment.
The patient presents with red papules with a smooth surface, suggesting a possible diagnosis of hemangioma or granuloma. Further investigations are needed for a definitive diagnosis.

This is a photo showing black-brown nodules the size of soybeans on the cheek, with nodules scattered isolatedly and with obvious keratinization on the surface. Based on the symptoms and signs, this is consistent with the clinical presentation of nodular prurigo. Nodular prurigo is a chronic, recurrent skin disease characterized by nodular skin lesions and itching. The etiology is not clear, but it may be related to immune reactions and genetic factors. Treatment often includes topical corticosteroids, antihistamines, and other medications. Patients should avoid scratching, keep the skin clean, and avoid irritants during treatment.

This patient presents with diffuse distribution of pinhead-sized red papules and plaques on the trunk and upper limbs, which have coalesced into large patches, consistent with the features of erythema multiforme. Erythema multiforme is a common skin condition often caused by infections, drug reactions, or other factors, characterized by rash and patches, typically accompanied by itching or burning sensation.

Based on the photo showing the adherence between the distal end of the nail plate and the nail bed with the presence of pterygium, the diagnosis is consistent with flat warts. Flat warts are a common skin condition, often caused by fungal infection or chronic eczema, commonly found in the nail area of the fingers. Treatment may include topical antifungal and anti-inflammatory medications.

In this case, based on the patient's symptoms description and physical examination findings, combined with the typical clinical manifestations of nodular erythema, the diagnosis is nodular erythema. Nodular erythema is a chronic, recurrent autoimmune skin disease characterized by scattered red or purplish nodules on the skin, often accompanied by itching or pain. Treatment typically involves the use of topical corticosteroids and oral antihistamines to relieve symptoms and control disease progression.

A large number of pink round papules and nodules of varying sizes are scattered on the mandible and neck. Consider: Neurofibromatosis. Further diagnosis requires a combination of family history, clinical manifestations, laboratory tests, etc.

This photo shows multiple lice eggs attached to the hair roots, indicating a diagnosis of pediculosis. Pediculosis is a condition caused by lice infestation on the scalp, commonly found in children and preschoolers. Patients may experience symptoms such as scalp itching, headache, and scalp inflammation. Treatment for pediculosis typically involves using pediculicides shampoo and cleaning hair, clothing, bedding, etc. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for more professional treatment guidance.

This photo shows redness and dilated, tortuous capillaries at the nostril. This is a typical presentation of telangiectasia. Telangiectasia is a common vascular skin disorder, often caused by defects in the elastic fibers of capillary walls or abnormalities in the muscular layer of the vessel walls. It commonly occurs on the face, especially the nostrils, cheeks, and chin, and is more prevalent in middle-aged and elderly individuals. Treatment options include laser therapy, radiofrequency treatment, and sclerotherapy, which have shown good efficacy.

The image shows extensive erythema and papules on the patient's forehead, with some papules appearing yellow and some already crusted. Based on the description, the patient is likely diagnosed with acne vulgaris. Acne vulgaris is a common chronic skin condition that typically occurs during adolescence, characterized by increased sebum production, follicular plugging, bacterial infection, and inflammation mainly on the face, chest, and back. Treatment usually involves topical medications, oral antibiotics, hormone therapy, etc. Timely treatment can help improve symptoms and prevent scarring.

In the lower extremities, there is dry skin with papules at the base, and scaling of the surface. No typical cauliflower-like changes are observed. These symptoms are consistent with eczema and further observation and diagnosis are needed.

This is an image showing skin lesions on the face, neck, and upper limbs, characterized by multiple red macules and papules the size of mung beans, some with scratch marks and scabs. Based on the characteristics and manifestations of the lesions, the diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus can be made.

This is a photo of the upper limb. The photo shows scattered round erythema with some vesicles on the upper limb. Based on the symptoms and signs, the preliminary diagnosis is severe type of erythema multiforme. Severe type of erythema multiforme is an inflammatory condition characterized by irregularly shaped erythema and vesicles on the skin, often accompanied by itching or burning sensation, commonly caused by allergic reactions or infections.

On this photo of the penis, red patches with scales are visible on the glans, which are indicative of psoriasis. Psoriasis is a common chronic autoimmune disease characterized by red patches, scales, and itching on the skin. The exact cause of psoriasis is not completely understood, but it may be related to factors such as genetics and immune system abnormalities. Treatment options for psoriasis include topical medications, phototherapy, and systemic medications, which should be chosen based on the severity of the condition and individual factors.

The photo shows multiple purplish-red nodules, which, along with clinical presentation and imaging findings, are consistent with the features of sarcoidosis. Sarcoidosis is an inflammatory disease that can affect multiple organs, commonly involving the lungs and lymph nodes. The exact cause is still unknown, and diagnosis relies on tissue biopsy. Treatment options include observation, medication, and surgery, tailored to the severity of the condition and the organs involved.

In the wrinkle area, there is a cock comb-like growth with a smooth surface, consistent with the clinical features of skin tag. Skin tags are common benign skin growths that typically occur due to overgrowth of skin cells in certain areas. This condition is usually benign and often does not require treatment unless symptomatic or for cosmetic reasons.

This photo shows a large area of ulceration on the patient's forehead, exposing subcutaneous tissue. Based on the characteristics of the lesion, the diagnosis is basal cell carcinoma, which is a common type of skin cancer that usually grows slowly but can be locally invasive. Treatment typically involves surgical excision, cryotherapy, radiation therapy, or topical medication. Timely treatment usually results in a good prognosis.

This is a picture showing a mucous cyst on the lip, likely caused by the retention of glandular secretions under the lip skin. Mucous cysts are usually benign and can be treated with surgical excision. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and management.

Based on the description and characteristics of the lesions, the diagnosis of nodular vasculitis can be made. Nodular vasculitis is a chronic nonspecific inflammatory skin disease commonly seen on both lower extremities. The exact cause is still unclear, but it may be related to immune abnormalities.

Based on the photo showing purple-red edematous patches around the eyelids, along with clinical symptoms and signs, the diagnosis of dermatomyositis is suspected. Dermatomyositis is an inflammatory skin disease characterized by redness, swelling, itching, and patches on the skin. This condition can cause discomfort and pain and requires a detailed skin examination and confirmation of diagnosis.

Based on the image, the patient has 3 cornified papules above the left ear with yellow crusts or adherent scales, which are consistent with the characteristics of actinic keratosis. Actinic keratosis is a common chronic skin condition caused by chronic sun exposure, characterized by excessive keratinization leading to the formation of cornified papules, commonly seen on sun-exposed areas such as the face, neck, and arms. Treatment involves sun avoidance, topical medications, and sometimes laser therapy when necessary.

The patient presents with dark brown patches and irregular borders, which are suggestive of melanoma. Melanoma is a malignant skin tumor originating from melanocytes. A definitive diagnosis of melanoma requires a comprehensive evaluation of the patient's medical history, skin features, and ancillary tests such as skin biopsy. Other skin conditions need to be ruled out before confirming the diagnosis.

This is a photo showing dark brown patches on the earlobe with clear borders, irregular edges, and smooth surface, consistent with features of malignant melanoma-like nevus. This condition is usually caused by malignant transformation of skin cells and requires prompt medical evaluation and treatment.

Behind the ear, there are brown keratotic papules, indicative of seborrheic keratosis. This condition is usually caused by the obstruction of hair follicle ducts, leading to excessive keratinization and the formation of papules. It is recommended to undergo a dermatological examination for a definitive diagnosis and follow the treatment plan provided by the physician.

The red patches, surface ulceration, crust formation, and capillary dilatation suggest a possibility of basal cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is the most common type of skin cancer, usually occurring on the head, neck, and upper limbs, presenting with symptoms such as red patches, ulcers, and crust formation. A definitive diagnosis would require a tissue biopsy.

The photo shows diffuse erythema and vesicles on the exposed areas of the back of both hands, which are consistent with the clinical presentation of dyshidrotic eczema. Dyshidrotic eczema is a chronic inflammatory skin condition that typically presents with dry, red, itchy skin, vesicles, or crusting. Treatment usually involves topical corticosteroid creams and moisturizers. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and treatment.

In this image of the upper limb, scattered red patches and papules with scales can be observed. These are characteristic features of chronic lichenoid eczema, a common chronic skin condition often caused by immune system abnormalities. Treatment typically involves topical medications and, when necessary, oral medications. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for professional guidance and treatment.

This is a photo of the head showing a smooth, yellow, semi-spherical raised lump on the scalp, consistent with a diagnosis of a trichilemmal cyst. Trichilemmal cysts are a common type of skin cyst that typically results from a blocked hair follicle. Treatment options may include local observation or surgical excision.

On the back of the hand, there are light-colored papules with keratosis on the surface and a central umbilication. This presentation is consistent with keratoacanthoma (squamous cell carcinoma) and further investigation is needed for confirmation.

This photo shows diffuse erythema and papules on the forehead of the patient, consistent with the diagnosis of rhinophyma. Rhinophyma is a common facial skin condition characterized by enlarged blood vessels, often seen in individuals with a history of chronic alcohol consumption or exposure to irritating substances.

Based on the patient's description, it is possible that there is yellow-brown thickening of the nail on the left thumb. Possible conditions to consider include paronychia, onychomycosis, etc. It is recommended to perform a detailed skin examination, inquire about the medical history, and if necessary, conduct fungal cultures or other relevant tests to confirm the diagnosis.

Psoriasis is a chronic, non-infectious autoimmune skin disease characterized by red patches covered with thick, silvery-white scales, often accompanied by itching and pain. As the condition progresses, the patches may enlarge and merge, severely affecting the patient's quality of life. Treatment for psoriasis includes topical medications, phototherapy, and systemic medications, and a personalized treatment plan should be developed based on the individual patient's condition.

The photo shows multiple tension blisters and large blisters on the back of the hand, accompanied by redness and pain, consistent with the clinical presentation of photocontact dermatitis. Photocontact dermatitis is a type of inflammatory skin condition usually caused by sunlight exposure, presenting with symptoms such as skin blisters, erythema, and edema. Treatment involves avoiding direct sunlight, topical corticosteroids, and antihistamines.

The photo shows the urine of a patient with porphyria appearing reddish-brown, emitting pink fluorescence under ultraviolet light. Porphyria is a genetic disorder characterized by a deficiency in specific enzymes in the body, leading to abnormal metabolism of porphyrins in the blood, ultimately causing them to precipitate in the urine and produce a reddish-brown color. Upon exposure to ultraviolet light, porphyrins in the urine exhibit fluorescence, appearing pink. This condition requires prompt diagnosis and treatment to alleviate symptoms and prevent complications.

The skin lesion presents as red-brown patches with clear and regular borders, considering seborrheic keratosis in combination with skin lesions in other areas. Seborrheic keratosis is a common chronic inflammatory skin disease that typically appears in areas with abundant sebum secretion such as the face and scalp, characterized by localized scaly skin lesions. It is more common in males in adolescence and early adulthood. Treatment may involve the use of topical medications and phototherapy.

The photo shows clear purple-red circular patches on the glans and coronal sulcus of the penis, which is a typical presentation of fixed drug eruption. Fixed drug eruption is a type of drug allergy reaction, characterized by localized skin inflammation and erythema that occurs after exposure to a specific medication. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for treatment and further evaluation.

Diagnosis: Irregularly shaped dark oval patches with satellite lesions on the edges, possibly melanoma, nevus, or seborrheic keratosis. It is recommended to undergo dermoscopy examination for a definitive diagnosis.
This is a photo taken in summer, showing multiple red patches on the thighs and shins with surrounding erythematous rings, resembling target-like lesions, indicative of erythema multiforme.

Based on the description of symptoms, the patient presents with a polarized erythematous patch in the hair-bearing area, accompanied by crusting and telangiectasia, which are indicative of basal cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is one of the most common malignant tumors of the skin, typically presenting as erythematous plaques or nodules in localized skin areas, commonly found on the head, neck, and face. A definitive diagnosis would require a skin biopsy to determine the specific pathological type and to formulate an appropriate treatment plan.

This is a photo of the hand showing erythematous patches with bullae and target-like lesions, consistent with a diagnosis of erythema multiforme. Erythema multiforme is a common self-limiting skin condition typically caused by allergic reactions, infections, or medications, presenting with irregularly shaped and varying sized erythematous patches with bullae. Treatment usually involves symptomatic relief and identification and management of underlying triggers.

This is a photo showing skin lesions on the trunk, characterized by bright red follicular papules that merge into patches. Based on the lesion features, along with clinical presentation and family history, the diagnosis of Darier's disease can be confirmed. Darier's disease is a genetic skin disorder characterized by abnormal keratinization leading to excessive keratin formation and the development of follicular cysts.

Based on the description provided, it is possible that the condition is a vascular skin disease. Neutrophilic dermatosis is a rare skin disease that typically presents with red patches, scattered distribution, flat elevations, and clear borders. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for a detailed skin examination before further investigations and diagnosis.

Scleromyxedema is a chronic inflammatory skin disease characterized by diffuse wax-like papules on the face or other areas, sometimes merging into plaques. This condition is often accompanied by edema and erythema, which may lead to skin hyperpigmentation and thickening. Treatment includes topical corticosteroids and oral anti-inflammatory medications. Regular follow-up visits to monitor disease progression and adjust treatment plans are crucial.

This is a photo of facial skin lesion showing pale red patches with pearly rolled edges, indicative of basal cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that usually grows slowly but can invade surrounding tissues if not treated promptly. It is recommended for the patient to seek medical attention for further diagnosis and treatment.

Flat lichen planus is a chronic skin disease characterized by red or purple flat-topped bumps on the skin with well-defined borders and a shiny, wax-like surface. The etiology of the disease is complex and may be related to factors such as genetics and immune system abnormalities. Lichen planus is generally not contagious, but it can persist and gradually spread. Treatment typically involves topical medications, phototherapy, and oral medications, but the condition is prone to recurrence and requires long-term management and monitoring.

In this patient, there are circular erythematous patches with smooth raised edges, consistent with the clinical presentation of granuloma annulare. Granuloma annulare is a common skin condition characterized by well-defined, localized erythematous plaques or nodules, often caused by viral infections or allergic reactions. Further histological examination is recommended for confirmation.

Z-shaped linear red patches appeared on the patient's trunk, and the diagnosis was considered to be nematode infection. Cutaneous nematodes is an inflammatory reaction and tissue damage caused by the invasion of the skin by linear miracidia. The diagnosis can be confirmed based on medical history and clinical manifestations such as linear erythema.

The photo shows a single, hemispherical nodular lesion with normal skin color, protruding on the skin surface, consistent with the clinical presentation of a dermatofibroma. Dermatofibroma is a common benign skin tumor that is usually painless and does not metastasize. Treatment options typically involve surgical excision.

Systemic lupus erythematosus is a chronic autoimmune disease that typically affects the skin, joints, kidneys, and other organs. On the scalp, it presents as irregular hair loss at the central parting line, infiltrative erythema, and disappearance of hair follicle openings. These manifestations can lead to sparse hair, hair loss, and even bald patches. Treatment often involves the use of anti-inflammatory drugs, immunosuppressants, and other medications for control.

The photo shows multiple papules or plaques on the patient's forearm and hand, covered with a thin layer of scales, diagnosed as lichen planus. Lichen planus is a chronic inflammatory skin disease commonly seen on the hands, feet, trunk, and other areas. It has a complex etiology, possibly related to genetics, immune system abnormalities, among other factors. Treatment typically involves topical steroids, phototherapy, among other methods.

This photo shows diffuse plaque psoriasis on the buttocks and lower limbs of the patient, covered with thick scales. This is a typical manifestation of psoriasis. Psoriasis is a chronic recurrent skin disease commonly found on the body joints, presenting as red patches with scales. The etiology of psoriasis is complex and involves various factors such as genetics, immunity, and environment.

These lesions on the skin present as dense, evenly distributed, smooth-surfaced, non-coalescing round papules or nodules, and are indicative of a rare skin condition known as cutaneous amyloidosis. This condition is typically caused by the deposition of amyloid proteins in the skin tissues, possibly with a genetic predisposition. Treatment usually involves a combination of medication and local care to alleviate symptoms and improve the patient's quality of life.
The patient presents with deep red semi-spherical papules with surrounding red patches, suggestive of a possible granuloma, but further investigations are required to confirm the diagnosis.

Based on the description of the photo showing dark brown patches with asymmetric shape, irregular borders, and uneven color on the foot dorsum, the diagnosis is malignant melanoma. Malignant melanoma is a type of malignant skin tumor characterized by the malignant proliferation of melanocytes. This tumor can grow rapidly and has a tendency to metastasize, so early diagnosis and treatment are crucial. Immediate medical attention is recommended for further evaluation and treatment.

In this photo of the elbow, the skin appears loose, showing signs of elastosis cutis, possibly due to prolonged friction or pressure causing damage to the elasticity of the skin. It is recommended to avoid prolonged friction or pressure, keep the skin moisturized, and consider using moisturizers and local massage to improve the symptoms.

The pink papules may be indicative of cutaneous neurofibromas, which are often caused by the overgrowth of nerve fibers. Neurofibromas are a relatively common neurogenic tumor that is usually benign but can sometimes be malignant. A definitive diagnosis typically requires a tissue biopsy.

This is a photo of the umbilicus. Visible granulation-like proliferation of tissue is observed. Based on the appearance of the lesion and the patient's medical history, the diagnosis is umbilical pyogenic granuloma. Pyogenic granuloma is an inflammatory growth commonly found on the skin and mucous membranes, often caused by bacterial infection. Treatment typically involves local antibiotics and surgical removal.
There are large circular red patches with yellow crusts on the top of the head, which raises suspicion of skin tumor and further investigation is needed for confirmation.

This is a photo of a lesion on the foot, characterized by a single grey-blue nodule protruding from the skin surface, with clear borders and a smooth surface, consistent with a blue nevus. Blue nevus is a common skin condition that is usually benign, but still requires differentiation from other skin lesions in clinical practice.

Based on the clinical presentation and medical history, the preliminary diagnosis for the facial red patches with yellow scales symmetrically on both cheeks is seborrheic dermatitis. Seborrheic dermatitis is a common chronic skin condition that is often caused by the overproduction of sebum from the sebaceous glands, leading to inflammation and scaling of the affected skin. Treatment typically involves topical medications and personalized skincare regimens.

The presence of black papules on the abdomen with gray-white crusts at the top is consistent with the clinical presentation of common warts. Common warts are common skin lesions caused by human papillomavirus (HPV) infection, typically presenting as papillary or flat lesions formed by local skin hyperplasia. The diagnosis of common warts usually requires a clinical examination by a dermatologist, and a tissue biopsy may be necessary if needed. Treatment options may include local medication, cryotherapy, or laser therapy. It is recommended to seek medical attention promptly for further evaluation and treatment.

This photo shows erythematous erosions and crusts on the exposed area of the back of the hand, typical manifestations of porphyria. Porphyria is a genetic disorder characterized by abnormal metabolism of porphyrins in the body, leading to photosensitivity of the skin and symptoms such as erythema, blisters, and crusts.

The photo shows erythematous papules and whitehead comedones consistent with the clinical presentation of folliculitis. Folliculitis is a common skin condition often caused by bacterial infection, and it can occur on the scalp, face, neck, and other areas. It is recommended to seek medical attention for appropriate treatment.

Scattered follicular papules on the forehead are consistent with the clinical features of acne, characterized by inflammatory papules around the hair follicles, often accompanied by increased sebum production and bacterial infection. Further investigation to rule out other skin conditions such as folliculitis is recommended.
The semi-spherical growth may be a sebaceous cyst or a lipoma. To confirm the diagnosis, a dermatologic ultrasound examination is recommended.

A nodule is present within the hairline with inflammatory changes in the center. Considerations include cysts or scars, further examination with dermatoscopy or soft tissue ultrasound is needed for a definitive diagnosis.
This is an image showing facial skin lesions with erythematous patches and papules of varying sizes on both cheeks, nose bridge, and earlobes, suggestive of sclerotic mucinosis.

Patients have dark red papules on the chest, which may be due to acne or scar formation. Acne is a common skin condition characterized by blockage of hair follicles, excess sebum production, and bacterial infection, commonly found on the face, chest, and back. Scars are formed due to excessive or insufficient collagen synthesis during the skin repair process after damage. It is recommended for the patient to seek further examination and diagnosis from a dermatologist.

Based on the characteristics of the lesion, the preliminary diagnosis is a melanocytic nevus. Melanocytic nevus is a common benign skin condition characterized by the overgrowth of melanocytes in the skin, often benign but should still be monitored for any changes.

At the areola, there are patchy white spots, and at the tip of the tail, there are red, thick, streaky patches, indicating a possible diagnosis of vitiligo. Vitiligo is a common chronic skin condition characterized by depigmented patches on the skin, commonly found on exposed areas such as the face, neck, hands, and feet. Diagnosis of vitiligo usually involves a combination of patient history, skin lesion presentation, and necessary laboratory tests.

This is a photo of the buttocks, where we can see large red erythematous papules with clear borders, consistent with the clinical presentation of allergic contact dermatitis. Allergic contact dermatitis is a common inflammatory skin condition, typically caused by an allergic reaction to an allergen the skin comes into contact with, presenting with symptoms such as rash, itching, and redness. Treatment involves avoiding contact with the allergen and using topical corticosteroids.

On the back of the finger, there is a red nodule with a ruptured surface. Considering the patient's medical history and clinical presentation, granuloma is suspected. Granuloma is an inflammatory condition that typically forms in response to chronic infection or foreign body reaction, characterized by localized tissue proliferation and damage. This condition is usually diagnosed through tissue biopsy and treatment may involve local anti-inflammatory medications, surgical excision, among other methods.

This photo shows symmetrical erythema, erosion, and exudation on both cheeks, consistent with the clinical presentation of eczema. Eczema is a common chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by symptoms such as redness, swelling, itching, erosion, and exudation. Treatment may involve the use of topical medications, maintaining skin hydration, and avoiding triggering factors.

This is a photo of the trunk area showing scattered erythematous papules. Combined with the patient's medical history and clinical presentation, the diagnosis of contact dermatitis is supported. Contact dermatitis is an inflammatory skin condition typically triggered by exposure to irritants or allergens, manifesting as redness, swelling, itching, and papules.

This is a photo showing skin lesions of the patient, characterized by erythematous patches with follicular papules. Based on the features of the lesions, the diagnosis is consistent with lichen simplex chronicus, a chronic non-infectious skin condition that typically presents with localized skin changes and itching.

Dorsal hand papules with cornification, suggestive of corns. Tissue pathology examination is recommended for a definitive diagnosis, ruling out other possibilities and establishing an effective treatment plan.

This is an image of a skin lesion on the back, showing a flat or raised pigmented patch on the left side of the back with regular borders and uniform color. Based on these characteristics, the most likely diagnosis is an epidermal nevus. This type of skin lesion is usually benign, but regular follow-up examinations are still needed to monitor for any changes.

The photo shows scattered pale yellow papules on the upper lip, consistent with Fordyce spots. Fordyce spots are a common lip condition characterized by yellowish or brownish spots on the lips, oral mucosa, and face. This condition is usually benign and attributed to sebaceous gland hyperplasia. It is advisable to monitor the lesions closely for any changes, and seek medical attention promptly if there is worsening of symptoms or any other discomfort.

Patients present with scattered dark red patches and papules on the upper limbs, suggestive of dermatitis. Further diagnostic investigations are needed. A histopathological examination is recommended to confirm the diagnosis.

In the patient's upper limb, there is a black elliptical spot, symmetrical in shape, with complete edges and uniform color, suggestive of a melanocytic nevus. Melanocytic nevus is a common skin lesion usually caused by pigment deposition in the superficial layer of the skin. In most cases, it is benign, but monitoring for any changes is important.
The presentation of skin ulcer with serous crust at the base, pink edges, and scaling suggests a possible skin tumor. To confirm the diagnosis, a pathological examination is recommended.

Based on the irregular large patches of erythema visible in the upper chest photo, along with the distribution of lesions, clinical symptoms, and signs, the diagnosis of dermatomyositis is considered. Dermatomyositis is an autoimmune disease characterized by symptoms such as redness, swelling, itching, and desquamation of the skin, sometimes accompanied by joint pain and muscle weakness. Confirmation of the diagnosis requires a combination of clinical presentation, skin biopsy, and laboratory test results.
In this photo, multiple pale yellow papules are visible on the mucous membrane, consistent with the diagnosis of sebaceous gland hyperplasia.

This photo shows several follicular papules on the limb, appearing red. The red halo is clearly visible on the edges of the papules, leading to a diagnosis of follicular papules with infection. Follicular papules are a common skin condition characterized by small papules around the hair follicles, often seen on the face, neck, chest, and other areas.

Behçet's disease, also known as Behçet's syndrome, is a chronic, recurrent autoimmune disease that can affect multiple areas including the oral mucosa, genitalia, eyes, and skin. It is characterized by ulcerative lesions, commonly found in the oral mucosa, presenting as ulcers with a white base and red borders. The diagnosis of Behçet's disease requires a combination of clinical symptoms, medical history, and pathological examination results. Treatment options may include local medication and immunosuppressive therapy to alleviate symptoms and manage the progression of the disease.

This is a photo showing yellow papules on the upper lip, which are either in millet-like or fused patch-like appearance, typical of sebaceous gland hyperplasia. Sebaceous gland hyperplasia is a common skin condition characterized by the appearance of small yellow or white papules on the skin, often seen on the face, lips, and other areas. The exact cause is not fully understood but may be related to overactive sebaceous gland secretion. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and treatment.

Reddish-brown patches may represent manifestations of skin tumors, and a histopathological examination is needed to confirm the diagnosis. Common skin tumors include benign ones such as moles, freckles, and malignant ones such as melanoma. Histopathological examination can help differentiate the nature of the tumor by observing the morphology and structure of tissue cells under a microscope, guiding subsequent treatment plans.

In a patient presenting with irregularly shaped, uniformly colored black papules on the trunk, differential diagnoses to consider include melanocytic nevus, pending evaluation for melanoma, and pending evaluation for seborrheic keratosis.

In this image of the upper limb, there are widespread keratotic papules with erythema around the hair follicles, resembling a chicken skin appearance, consistent with the clinical presentation of follicular keratosis. Follicular keratosis is a common chronic keratinizing skin disease characterized by rough skin, excessive keratin formation leading to papules, often seen on the upper arms, thighs, and other areas. Treatment may involve the use of keratolytic agents and topical keratolytic agents.

This photo shows a dark red plaque with slight infiltration and some areas of crusting, which is consistent with the characteristics of basal cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that typically grows slowly, rarely metastasizes, but if not treated promptly, it may invade surrounding tissues. Treatment usually involves surgical excision, cryotherapy, radiation therapy, etc.

This is a photo of the lower limbs showing multiple smooth reddish-brown papules, consistent with the typical presentation of pigmentary urticaria. Pigmentary urticaria is a skin condition characterized by the appearance of red or brown papules or patches on the skin, often accompanied by itching.

This description fits the characteristics of a melanocytic nevus, which is a common skin lesion that is usually benign but still requires further evaluation by a dermatologist for a definitive diagnosis. Additionally, considering the features of excessive keratinization in the lesion, the possibility of seborrheic keratosis should also be considered. Seborrheic keratosis is a common inflammatory skin condition that can usually be confirmed through examination by a dermatologist. It is recommended to seek medical attention early for further evaluation and treatment.

Based on the irregularly shaped black protrusion with uneven color in the upper jaw shown in the photo, in conjunction with clinical symptoms and photo findings, there is a strong suspicion of oral squamous cell carcinoma. Squamous cell carcinoma is one of the most common malignant tumors in the oral cavity, often presenting as ulcers, masses, and other symptoms. Confirmation of the diagnosis requires further pathological examination.

The photo shows several red papules on the chest, which are typically caused by coming into contact with substances that trigger an allergic reaction, such as chemicals, plants, medications, or other external irritants. Symptoms of contact dermatitis include rash, itching, burning sensation, and localized swelling. Treatment usually involves avoiding contact with the allergen, using topical corticosteroids, and antihistamines. Further investigation is needed to identify the causative agent for appropriate management.

Based on the characteristics of the lesion, the patient is diagnosed with nodular sclerosis, a rare connective tissue disease commonly affecting the skin of the face and neck. It typically presents with pigmented patches and nodules.

By observing the photo and making a diagnosis, the patient is confirmed to have a condition of epidermal dysplasia, a rare genetic disorder that typically presents with abnormal development of the skin or mucous membranes, which may affect the normal development of the scalp, nails, teeth, among other areas.

The photo shows scattered papules on the back of the hand. Based on the clinical symptoms and signs, the most likely diagnosis is contact dermatitis. Contact dermatitis is a common skin inflammation typically caused by skin exposure to irritants or allergens, presenting with symptoms such as redness, swelling, itching, and rash. Treatment involves avoiding contact with irritants and using topical corticosteroids and other medications. It is advisable to consult a dermatologist for further evaluation and treatment.

The photo shows symptoms such as redness, erosion, exudation, and scabbing on the skin, which are consistent with the clinical manifestations of eczema. Eczema is a common inflammatory skin condition, often caused by allergic reactions or inflammation of the skin. Treatment for eczema includes the use of topical medications, avoiding irritants, keeping the skin clean and moisturized. In clinical practice, it is also necessary to rule out other skin conditions that may cause similar symptoms to confirm the diagnosis.

The patient presents with a smooth, dark red papule on the skin measuring approximately 8mm×6mm in size. Based on the characteristics of the lesion, skin fibroma is considered as a possible diagnosis, but further investigation is needed to confirm. It is recommended to undergo a skin dermoscopy examination. Skin dermoscopy is a method that helps doctors diagnose skin lesions by magnifying and illuminating them, allowing for a more accurate determination of the nature of the lesion and aiding in treatment planning.

This image shows two black uneven protrusions on the trunk, and based on the characteristics of the lesion, the preliminary diagnosis is cystic basal cell carcinoma. This type of cancer usually originates from the basal cells of the skin and can occur in any area of the skin, commonly found on the head and neck. Cystic basal cell carcinoma typically grows slowly, but if not treated promptly, it may spread and invade surrounding tissues.

This image shows a dark brown nodule with white halo surrounding it, with well-defined borders and clear demarcation, consistent with a halo nevus. Halo nevus is a common skin lesion characterized by overgrowth of pigment-producing cells on the skin surface, most often benign in nature. However, periodic monitoring is sometimes necessary to watch for potential malignant transformation.

Facial, umbilical, and perineal red patches with desquamation and erosion are present. Considering the clinical presentation, pyoderma gangrenosum is suspected. Pyoderma gangrenosum is a rare autoimmune disease characterized by skin ulcers, pain, and inflammation, often affecting the face, trunk, groin, and limbs. Diagnosis requires a combination of clinical symptoms, skin biopsy results, and exclusion of other diseases. Treatment mainly involves skin care, oral or topical steroids, immunosuppressants, and other medications.

The patient developed clusters of small smooth papules behind the ears, which was considered: scleromyxedema. Further histopathological examination is recommended to confirm the diagnosis and exclude the possibility of malignant disease.

Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is a rare genetic connective tissue disorder characterized by skin hyperextensibility, joint hypermobility, and easy scarring. It is often caused by mutations in collagen genes, leading to abnormalities in connective tissue. Patients typically present with loose skin, easy bruising, and a tendency to scar easily. Treatment involves symptom management, rehabilitation therapy, and psychological support. Patients require regular follow-up and management to prevent complications.

The patient presents with severe swelling and scaling on the lower limbs, consistent with the clinical features of lymphedema. Lymphedema is a condition characterized by localized fluid retention in tissues due to obstruction of the lymphatic system, commonly affecting the lower limbs. Further evaluation is recommended to confirm the diagnosis and initiate appropriate treatment.

Based on the photo showing ulceration and erosion on the lips, the diagnosis of erythema multiforme is suspected. Erythema multiforme is a common skin condition characterized by symmetrically distributed red patches, blisters, erosion, and ulcers. The exact cause of this condition is not clear yet, but it may be related to genetics, immune abnormalities, and viral infections. Treatment usually involves topical anti-inflammatory and analgesic medications, while severe cases may require oral steroids or immunosuppressants. Seeking timely medical attention and following the doctor's advice can effectively control the condition.

This is an image of a skin lesion on the forearm, presenting as annular erythema with scales on the inner edge and clear borders. Based on the characteristics of the lesion, a preliminary diagnosis of tinea corporis is made, which is a common skin condition often caused by fungal infection, requiring further examination and treatment.

In this case, based on the symptoms described and the image showing dark red erythematous patches in the armpit, the patient is likely diagnosed with eczema. Eczema is a common skin condition characterized by symptoms such as dry skin, itching, redness, swelling, blistering, and crusting. When diagnosing eczema, doctors usually conduct a comprehensive evaluation based on the patient's medical history, clinical presentation, and necessary laboratory tests.

The photo shows multiple blue-purple nodules on the skin, based on the characteristics of the lesions, the possibility of sarcoidosis can be considered. Sarcoidosis is an inflammatory disease that can affect multiple organs, including the skin. A definitive diagnosis of sarcoidosis typically requires a comprehensive evaluation combining clinical symptoms, imaging findings, and laboratory tests.

The patient presents with scattered white patches on the trunk with black and brown spots in the center, suggestive of a diagnosis of a halo nevus. A halo nevus is a common skin condition characterized by uneven skin pigmentation, often appearing as irregularly colored patches or spots on the skin, commonly found on the face, neck, trunk, and other areas. When diagnosing a halo nevus, it is important for healthcare providers to differentiate it from other skin conditions such as freckles and pigmented lesions, and in some cases, a skin biopsy may be required for a definitive diagnosis.

Black melanocytic nevus is a common skin lesion, typically presenting as pigmented spots or patches on the skin in shades of brown or black. These nevi are usually round or oval in shape, with smooth surfaces and clear borders. While melanocytic nevi are usually benign, they can potentially progress to malignant melanoma in certain cases, highlighting the importance of regular examination and monitoring.

This is a photo of penile skin showing Fordyce spots, characterized by white papules arranged in a corona-like pattern on the glans penis, with a smooth surface. Fordyce spots are a common benign condition that typically does not require treatment. It is recommended to monitor the lesion regularly for any changes and follow medical advice for further treatment or evaluation to rule out other possible conditions.

This photo shows purplish-red edematous erythema on the eyelids and cheeks of the patient, consistent with the clinical features of dermatomyositis. Dermatomyositis is an autoimmune disease characterized by skin inflammation and muscle weakness, commonly affecting areas such as the face, neck, and limbs. Prompt diagnosis and treatment are crucial to alleviate symptoms and prevent disease progression.

The photograph shows red patches raised on the skin surface with clustered pustules around it, consistent with the clinical presentation of cutaneous sporotrichosis. This condition is a fungal skin infection commonly found in moist and warm environments, and it can be diagnosed through clinical symptoms and fungal culture.

The photo shows multiple dark red lumps on the lower limb, forming obvious protrusions, which, combined with the clinical presentation, is suspicious for Kaposi's sarcoma. Kaposi's sarcoma is a malignant tumor that typically occurs in the skin and soft tissues, commonly seen in immunocompromised patients. Diagnosis usually requires a tissue biopsy for confirmation.

Erythema multiforme is an acute and recurrent inflammatory disease of the skin and mucous membranes. Its characteristic rash consists of central red papules, surrounded by edematous pale red rings, with outermost erythema. It is often accompanied by itching or pain. Erythema multiforme can be caused by various factors such as infection, drug reactions, and allergies. Severe cases may present with systemic symptoms like fever and joint pain. Treatment involves identifying and addressing the underlying cause, and severe cases may require oral or intravenous anti-inflammatory medications. Patients are advised to seek medical attention promptly and avoid self-medication.

The photo of the face shows a large area of depigmentation around the lips, which, combined with clinical manifestations and photo characteristics, is diagnosed as folliculitis on the lips. Folliculitis is a common skin condition, typically caused by bacterial infection, characterized by inflammation around the hair follicles and pigmentation.

The photo shows black patches on the toe, with a rough surface and obvious keratosis. Based on the characteristics of the lesion, the preliminary diagnosis is melanocytic nevus. Melanocytic nevus is a common skin lesion, usually caused by the overgrowth of melanocytes in the skin. In most cases, it is benign, but regular monitoring is still needed to rule out the possibility of malignant transformation.

Scleroderma is a rare chronic autoimmune disease characterized by fibrosis and hardening of the skin and internal organs. It can be divided into localized scleroderma and systemic scleroderma, with symptoms including skin hardening, thickening, and tightness. Diagnosis of scleroderma requires a comprehensive evaluation of clinical symptoms, physical signs, and laboratory findings. Treatment mainly focuses on symptom management and disease progression control. Long-term close follow-up and regular monitoring are necessary after confirmation of the diagnosis.

This photo shows purple-red patches with slight scaling on the extensor surface of the joints, which, combined with clinical symptoms and signs, are consistent with the diagnosis of dermatomyositis. Dermatomyositis is a chronic inflammatory skin disease characterized by symptoms such as erythema, scaling, and itching, which can affect joint movement and function. Treatment typically includes topical corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, and occasionally physical therapy.

This photo of the hand shows diffuse erythema, partial skin sclerosis and atrophy, and smooth skin without sweating, which are characteristic features of scleroderma. Scleroderma is a chronic, progressive connective tissue disease characterized by fibrosis and hardening of the skin, which can also affect internal organs, leading to various symptoms and complications.

This is a photo of the eye, showing an irregular-shaped black patch on the conjunctiva. Based on the characteristics and presentation of the lesion, along with the patient's medical history and clinical examination, the diagnosis is ocular melanoma. Ocular melanoma is a type of eye tumor that can potentially affect vision and eye health, requiring timely treatment and follow-up.

This is an image of skin lesions on the trunk, with pink, soft nodules of varying sizes distributed densely on the trunk and upper limbs. Based on the characteristics of the lesions, the patient may be diagnosed with neurofibromatosis. Neurofibromatosis is a common type of nerve sheath tumor that is usually benign and results from the overgrowth of peripheral nerve tissue. These tumors can form around any nerve tissue in the body, often in the skin, soft tissues, and bones. In neurofibromatosis, multiple neurofibromas can be seen on the patient's body surface, and these tumors may present with different shapes and colors during their growth.

The patient presents with large areas of edematous erythema with clear borders on the left eyelid, cheeks, lower jaw, and neck, indicative of dermatomyositis. Dermatomyositis is an inflammatory skin condition characterized by symptoms such as skin swelling, erythema, and itching. The etiology of dermatomyositis is diverse and may be related to immune system abnormalities, infections, medications, among other factors. Treatment typically involves the use of anti-inflammatory medications, topical treatments, and addressing the underlying cause. It is recommended for the patient to seek medical attention promptly for further diagnosis and treatment.

The patient presents with a protruding mass around the anal area, with a surface that is uneven, white on the top, and black on the bottom. These symptoms suggest a possible diagnosis of scabies, a common skin parasitic infection. Patients may experience itching, pain, and other discomfort.

Purple blood blisters may indicate a subcutaneous hematoma, which is often caused by the rupture of small blood vessels under the skin, leading to the accumulation of blood. Purple patches may suggest melanoma, a malignant tumor that usually originates from melanocytes in the skin. A detailed skin examination and further tests are necessary for a proper diagnosis.

Large brown patches with hair on the trunk are consistent with seborrheic keratosis. Seborrheic keratosis is a common skin condition characterized by well-defined brown patches with hair on the surface, most of which are benign. It is recommended to regularly observe changes in the shape, color, and size of the lesion, and seek medical attention promptly if any abnormal changes occur for further evaluation and treatment.

This is a clinical presentation of squamous cell carcinoma, characterized by an irregularly bordered infiltrative erythema on the skin, covered with scales and crusts. Squamous cell carcinoma is a common type of skin cancer that typically originates from the squamous cells of the epidermis. Early symptoms may include local skin changes such as erythema and desquamation, progressing to ulceration, bleeding, and other symptoms. A definitive diagnosis requires a comprehensive evaluation considering clinical manifestations, histopathological findings, and other factors.
This is a picture of a skin lesion on the right side of the neck, showing dark red patches with a few dark red papules on top, consistent with the presentation of a port-wine stain.

Black pigmented patch with central area missing, differential diagnosis includes skin tumor, seborrheic keratosis, and melanoma. A histopathological examination is recommended for a definitive diagnosis.

This facial photo shows multiple dark brown pigmented spots and patches, along with areas of hypopigmentation. Based on the symptoms and characteristics of the skin lesions, the diagnosis is likely to be pigmentary demarcation lines, a common chronic pigmentary skin condition. It typically presents as uneven skin pigmentation, dark patches, and associated keratosis. Treatment options may include topical whitening agents and laser therapy.

On the face of the patient in the photo, scattered erythematous papules can be seen in the central part, indicative of rosacea. Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory condition that mainly affects women in their adolescence. The exact cause is not clear, but it is thought to be related to overactive sebaceous glands, bacterial infection, and immune reactions. Treatment usually involves topical medications and oral antibiotics. Seeking timely medical attention and following the prescribed treatment can effectively manage the condition.

On this photo of the back, we can see scattered black melanocytic nevi with a halo of depigmentation around them. Based on the characteristics of the lesions, along with clinical presentation and imaging studies, the diagnosis is a compound nevus with irregular borders, uneven color, and size. Close follow-up observation is required to rule out malignant melanoma.

This X-ray shows multiple subcutaneous calcium deposits, consistent with the clinical presentation of dermatomyositis. Dermatomyositis is a rare disease characterized by the deposition of calcium salts in the skin tissues, leading to symptoms such as skin hardening and patchy changes.

White irregular patches are visible at the eyebrow tips in this facial photo. Based on the symptoms and presentation, the preliminary diagnosis is vitiligo. Vitiligo is a chronic, recurrent skin depigmentation disease, typically characterized by localized or widespread white patches on the skin. It commonly affects areas such as the face, hands, and trunk. The etiology is complex and may be related to factors such as autoimmune, genetic, and environmental influences. Treatment should be tailored to the individual patient and may involve medication or phototherapy, with close monitoring of the condition.

The patient presents with multiple purple-red flat-topped polygonal papules on the back of the hand, with a waxy shine, consistent with the clinical features of flat warts. Flat warts are a skin condition caused by fungal infection, commonly found on the hands and feet, with lesions appearing flat and well-defined, often accompanied by itching. Treatment typically involves topical antifungal medications and maintaining local cleanliness and dryness. It is recommended that the patient seek medical attention promptly and follow the doctor's treatment instructions.

On this photo of the trunk, a circular white nodule can be seen, which is diagnosed as a trichilemmal tumor. Trichilemmal tumors are common skin tumors that typically present as round or oval lesions on the skin surface, sometimes appearing white or pinkish. Treatment usually involves surgical removal, and in cases of malignant trichilemmal tumors, further treatment with radiation therapy or chemotherapy may be necessary.

On the back of the hand, there are red flat papules with scales on the surface, which may indicate a skin disorder. Further investigations, such as a skin biopsy, are necessary to confirm the diagnosis. It is recommended to consult a dermatologist for a detailed assessment and diagnosis. Possible diagnoses include squamous cell carcinoma, cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, among others. The treatment plan will depend on the final diagnosis.

There are black oval patches on the sole of the foot, with indistinct borders and symmetrical shapes, which are typical characteristics of a melanocytic nevus. Regular monitoring for any changes is recommended.

This is a photo of a skin lesion. It shows bright red papules and patches with clear borders, consistent with the clinical presentation of a lymphatic malformation. Lymphatic malformations are common vascular tumors that are usually caused by abnormal development of lymphatic vessels. This condition is typically benign but may require treatment in some cases.

Under the red patches on both shins there are nodules, suggesting nodular vasculitis. Nodular vasculitis is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by skin redness, nodules, and ulcers, often accompanied by joint pain and muscle pain. Detailed physical examination and laboratory tests are recommended for diagnosis and treatment planning.

The image shows a solitary, hemispherical, dark red nodule with central necrosis and ulceration, which is consistent with features of fungal infection based on clinical presentation and imaging characteristics. The diagnosis of fungal disease usually requires further pathological examination and fungal culture to confirm the type of pathogen, guiding subsequent treatment strategies. Common fungal infections include candidiasis, aspergillosis, etc., with treatment primarily focused on antifungal medications. Clinicians also need to consider the patient's immune status, medical history, and the location of the diagnosis to provide comprehensive treatment.

Z-shaped linear red patches appeared on the soles of the patient's feet, and the diagnosis was considered to be nematode infection. Cutaneous nematodes is an inflammatory reaction and tissue damage caused by the invasion of skin by linear miracidia. The diagnosis can be confirmed based on medical history and clinical manifestations such as linear erythema.

The presented symptoms are characteristic of follicular keratosis, manifested by large patches of erythema with pinpoint-sized follicular keratotic papules. Follicular keratosis is a genetic skin disorder primarily caused by abnormal keratinization of hair follicle openings leading to excessive skin keratinization. This condition typically appears in adolescence and commonly affects areas such as the face, neck, and chest. Treatment options may include topical keratolytic agents, keratosis regulators, or laser therapy. If the symptoms significantly impact quality of life, it is advisable to consult a dermatologist for a more detailed evaluation and treatment.

This image shows symptoms on the upper limb of the patient, presenting scattered round erythematous patches with vesicles in some of them. Based on the symptoms and description, the preliminary diagnosis is a bullous skin disease, possibly herpes skin disease or pemphigus. It is recommended that the patient seek medical attention promptly for further examination and confirmation.
The patient's skin was spherical pink and nodular in shape, consistent with dermatofibroma, and surgical removal was recommended.

This is a photo of an infant's face showing erythematous papules with slight scaling, consistent with seborrheic dermatitis. Seborrheic dermatitis is a common inflammatory skin condition often caused by excess sebum production and fungal infection, commonly seen in infants and young children. Treatment involves keeping the skin clean, using topical antifungal agents, and possibly topical steroids. Early treatment can usually help alleviate symptoms and prevent complications.

Based on the diffuse hyperkeratosis and desquamation on the trunk and limbs in the full-body photo, along with the clinical history and characteristics of the skin lesions, the diagnosis of scabies is established. Scabies is a contagious skin infestation caused by the Sarcoptes scabiei mite, presenting with symptoms such as itching, erythema, papules, and desquamation. The excretions produced by the mites on the skin surface lead to inflammatory reactions. Treatment often involves the use of topical or oral medications to kill the mites, along with disinfection of the household and close contacts.

This is a photo of a male neck with irregularly shaped pigmented patches, scattered vascular tumors, and verrucous keratosis. The diagnosis is dyschromatosis symmetrica hereditaria. Dyschromatosis symmetrica hereditaria is a chronic skin disorder characterized by localized pigmentation, thickening, and roughness of the skin, often accompanied by keratosis and vascular dilation. Treatment may include topical medications and laser therapy.

This is a picture showing a lesion on the lip skin, characterized by infiltrative pale red patches with surface erosion, ulceration, necrosis, and crusting, as well as an uneven surface. Based on the features of the lesion, the preliminary diagnosis is squamous cell carcinoma of the lip.

This photo shows a dome-shaped nodule on the skin surface, surrounded by dilated and tortuous branching blood vessels, which is a typical presentation of basal cell carcinoma. Basal cell carcinoma is one of the most common malignant tumors of the skin, typically growing slowly, but if left untreated, it can invade surrounding tissues and organs, requiring early diagnosis and treatment.
